title: obione orbicularis
intro: Obione orbicularis, commonly known as Sea Purslane, is a succulent plant species that thrives in coastal and salt-tolerant environments. It has been utilized for centuries due to its impressive array of health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory properties, which can aid in reducing pain and swelling, as well as its antioxidant properties, which help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. The therapeutic actions of Obione orbicularis include its ability to act as an anti-inflammatory agent, an antimicrobial agent, and an antiseptic agent, making it a valuable treatment for wounds, skin irritations, and infections. The bioactive constituents responsible for these actions include flavonoids, alkaloids, and glycosides, which have been shown to possess potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties.
parts: Obione orbicularis has many medicinal parts, such as the roots, leaves, and whole plant, which are used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The roots of Obione orbicularis contain flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The leaves of the plant are rich in phenolic compounds, including quercetin and kaempferol, which have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. Additionally, the whole plant contains sesquiterpenes, which have been found to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, making it a valuable medicinal resource.
side_effects: Obione orbicularis can have side effects if used improperly, such as causing allergic reactions, skin irritation, and increased heart rate due to its high concentration of toxic compounds. In rare cases, excessive consumption can lead to nausea, vomiting, and even liver damage. Some users have reported headaches and dizziness after using the plant in its unprocessed form. If not handled or prepared correctly, Obione orbicularis can also cause anaphylaxis, a severe and potentially life-threatening allergic reaction.
